planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Fraser Magnolia is an understory tree that performs best in sheltered positions with protection from late spring frosts that can damage the large, showy flower buds.

directions: Fraser Magnolia is a deciduous native magnolia of the southern Appalachians, native to the rich, moist cove forests and ravine slopes from Virginia and West Virginia south to Georgia and Alabama, immediately recognizable by its enormous leaves (up to 18 inches long) with distinctive ear-like lobes (auricles) at the base — the largest leaves of any magnolia native to the southern mountains. In May and June it produces large, cup-shaped, creamy-white, fragrant flowers 8–10 inches across that emit a sweet, lemony fragrance, followed in fall by striking pink to red cone-like fruit clusters that split to reveal bright red seeds, providing ornamental interest from spring through fall. Fraser Magnolia is a shade-tolerant understory tree that grows naturally in the deepest, richest, most sheltered cove forest conditions alongside cucumber magnolia, yellow buckeye, and basswood, and it translates beautifully to shaded woodland gardens in zones 5–8. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade, protecting from prevailing wind to prevent the large leaves from tearing.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Ashe Magnolia blooms within 3–5 years from seed — much faster than most magnolias — and is appropriate for smaller gardens where Bigleaf Magnolia (Magnolia macrophylla) would be too large.

directions: Ashe Magnolia is one of the rarest magnolias in North America, a critically imperiled native shrub or small tree endemic to the steep, moist, shaded ravines and bluffs of the Florida Panhandle in a handful of counties in far northwest Florida and adjacent Alabama, where fewer than 20 wild populations survive. Despite its rarity in the wild, Ashe Magnolia is relatively easy to grow in cultivation and is widely offered by specialty native plant nurseries; it is often treated as a dwarf or subspecies of the closely related Bigleaf Magnolia (Magnolia macrophylla) but is considered a distinct species by most authorities. The enormous, white, bowl-shaped flowers (12–14 inches across) with purple-spotted centers appear in April and May and are among the most spectacular of any magnolia — attracting native bees and beetles as pollinators. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade and protect from drying winds that damage the enormous leaves; it is significantly smaller and faster-blooming than M. macrophylla, making it more suitable for the typical home garden.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Pyramid Magnolia is one of the rarest and least-known native magnolias and should only be sourced from specialty native plant nurseries.

directions: Pyramid Magnolia is a rare native deciduous magnolia of the southeastern coastal plain, found in scattered populations in Georgia, Florida, Alabama, Mississippi, and Texas, growing in moist, rich, well-drained soil in the understory of mixed hardwood forest — a much more restricted habitat than the closely related Fraser Magnolia (Magnolia fraseri), which it resembles closely but with a more pyramidal form and consistently smaller stature. White, cup-shaped flowers 3–4 inches across appear in April and May before or with the emerging leaves; the large, auriculate (ear-lobed) leaves and attractive pink-red fruit clusters provide multi-season interest similar to its Appalachian relatives. Among the least-known of the native magnolias in horticulture despite being well-suited to zone 7–9 gardens, Pyramid Magnolia is increasingly available from specialty native nurseries as interest in deciduous native magnolias grows. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, deeply amended soil in partial shade; protect from strong winds that damage the large leaves.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, deep soil in full sun to partial shade. Select disease-resistant cultivars ('Valley Forge', 'Princeton', 'New Harmony') to reduce susceptibility to Dutch Elm Disease; choose open sites with good air circulation.

directions: American Elm was for much of the 19th and early 20th centuries the most important street and shade tree in eastern North America, the classic 'cathedral' tree whose interlocking vase-shaped canopies arched over village streets from New England to the Great Plains in an iconic display of American civic beauty — until Dutch Elm Disease (Ophiostoma ulmi), introduced from Europe in the 1930s, devastated the species throughout most of its natural range and removed it from the American streetscape within a few decades. Today, disease-resistant cultivars ('Valley Forge', 'Princeton', 'New Harmony') have restored American Elm to viability in the landscape and are the preferred planting choice for new trees; these cultivars maintain the original graceful, vase-shaped habit that made the species so beloved, with resistance to DED ranging from moderate to strong. American Elm is adaptable to a wide range of soils from wet bottomlands to dry uplands and tolerates urban conditions, road salt, compaction, and air pollution better than most native trees. Plant disease-resistant cultivars in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil; provide adequate space for the wide-spreading mature canopy.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun to partial shade. Slippery Elm is moderately susceptible to Dutch Elm Disease but less so than American Elm; it is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions including clay, limestone soils, and occasionally flooded areas.

directions: Slippery Elm is a large, somewhat coarser native elm than American Elm, native to rich, moist forest slopes, bottomlands, and upland woods throughout eastern North America from Quebec to Florida and west to the Dakotas, named for the distinctive slippery, mucilaginous quality of its inner bark, which has been used medicinally for centuries as a demulcent (soothing agent) for sore throats, coughs, and gastrointestinal inflammation — the FDA has recognized the inner bark as a safe and effective over-the-counter demulcent. The rough, sandpaper-textured leaves are distinctive and diagnostic for the species; the reddish, hairy leaf buds in late winter are among the first signs of life in the spring forest. While moderately susceptible to Dutch Elm Disease, Slippery Elm is adaptable to a wider range of soil conditions than American Elm, tolerating clay, limestone, and occasionally wet soils. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; the coarse texture and broad shade make it a valuable native alternative to cultivated elms.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to alkaline, deep soil in full sun. Cedar Elm tolerates alkaline limestone soils, compaction, drought, and urban conditions better than most other elms and is the preferred native elm for landscapes in central and south Texas.

directions: Cedar Elm is the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the native elms, native to the river bottoms, limestone floodplains, and upland limestone woods of Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Louisiana, where it frequently grows adjacent to cedar (Juniperus species) — hence its common name. Its most distinctive habit is bloom timing: unlike all other North American elms that bloom in late winter before leaf-out, Cedar Elm blooms in late summer and fall (August–October), producing tiny, inconspicuous flowers followed by small, papery, winged seeds; this unusual timing means it can be confused with a disease-stricken elm (sparse fall foliage is normal, not worrying). A tough, adaptable native shade tree that tolerates the alkaline limestone soils, compaction, and summer heat of Texas landscapes with exceptional resilience, Cedar Elm is one of the most widely recommended native shade trees for central and south Texas gardens. Grow in full sun in any well-drained to moist soil; it adapts readily to both alkaline limestone and acidic sandy soils.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral alluvial or bottomland soil in full sun. American Sycamore grows best in deep, moist, fertile soils near water and becomes a massive tree that requires ample space; do not plant near structures, sewer lines, or small yards.

directions: American Sycamore is one of the largest deciduous trees in North America — with trunk diameters commonly reaching 6–8 feet and old specimens exceeding 10 feet in diameter — native to the moist, rich alluvial soils of river bottoms, floodplains, and stream terraces throughout eastern North America from Maine to Florida and west to Kansas and Texas, where its massive, gleaming white upper trunk (revealed as the outer gray-brown bark sheds in large patches) makes it visible from great distances and impossible to mistake for any other native tree. The distinctive mottled, ivory-white, cream, and gray trunk bark is among the most ornamental features of any North American tree, and the large, maple-like leaves create deep, cooling shade in summer; the spherical, golf-ball-sized seed heads hang through winter and are consumed by a variety of birds including American goldfinches. Wood ducks, chimney swifts, and other cavity-nesting species prize the large hollow cavities that develop in old sycamores. Plant in deep, moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil near water features, streams, or in large park settings; it is not appropriate for typical residential lots given its ultimately massive size.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants (or hardwood cuttings rooted in moist soil) in spring in moist, well-drained, sandy or loamy alluvial soil in full sun. Eastern Cottonwood grows extraordinarily fast — up to 6–8 feet per year — but is short-lived (60–100 years) and prone to branch breakage in ice storms; plant male cultivars to avoid the cottony seed mess of female trees.

directions: Eastern Cottonwood is the fastest-growing native tree in North America, capable of adding 6–8 feet of height per year in moist, fertile, alluvial soils — a growth rate no other native tree approaches — and is native to the river bars, floodplains, and moist bottomlands of virtually every drainage system in eastern and central North America from Quebec to Florida and west to the Rockies. The triangular (deltoid) leaves tremble and shimmer in the slightest breeze due to their flattened petioles, creating a distinctive rustling sound audible at considerable distances; in late spring, female trees produce enormous quantities of cottony-tufted seeds that fill the air like midsummer snow, settling on every surface and creating what many observers find an enchanting spectacle and others find a nuisance. An important pioneer tree for streambank stabilization and riparian restoration, Eastern Cottonwood provides critical early-successional habitat for many birds and insects. Plant male cultivars to avoid seed dispersal; provide full sun and deep, moist soil near water features or for rapid windbreak and privacy screening.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants or root hardwood cuttings (12–18 inch stem sections planted directly in moist soil) in early spring. Black Willow establishes exceptionally rapidly and begins producing root anchorage within weeks; it is the preferred native species for immediate streambank and erosion control in moist to wet sites.

directions: Black Willow is the largest native willow in North America and the most important native tree for the immediate stabilization of eroding streambanks, native to the moist to wet, sandy and gravelly alluvial soils of rivers, streams, and floodplains throughout eastern North America from New Brunswick to Florida and west to Montana and New Mexico. Its aggressive, fast-establishing root system grips unstable bank soil within weeks of planting, and hardwood cuttings root readily when simply pushed into moist streambank soil in early spring — making it the fastest and cheapest native solution for stream restoration and bank protection. The pendulous, narrow, lance-shaped leaves create a graceful, weeping appearance over water; catkins appear in early spring, providing one of the season's earliest pollen sources for native bees emerging from winter dormancy. Black Willow is short-lived (40–60 years) and prone to branch breakage in ice and wind storms, but its rapid establishment and wildlife value (woodpecker habitat, caterpillar host plant) make it an invaluable native pioneer for wet, disturbed sites where quick establishment is the priority.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in fall in well-drained, acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun. Shortleaf Pine adapts to a wide range of well-drained, acidic soils from dry sand to moist clay loam, and is one of the most widely distributed southern pines — tolerating both droughty uplands and moderately moist conditions.

directions: Shortleaf Pine is one of the most widely distributed southern pines, native to 22 states from New York and New Jersey south to Florida and west to Texas and Oklahoma, growing in dry to moderately moist, acidic, well-drained to somewhat poorly drained soils across a remarkable variety of upland and foothill conditions — a broader soil and climate tolerance than almost any other southern pine. Its most distinctive adaptive trait is the ability to regenerate from dormant basal buds (called 'root sprouts') after top-killing fire or mechanical damage — a trait shared with longleaf pine but uncommon among other pines — that allows it to recover from disturbance without relying solely on seeding. Shortleaf Pine has been a major timber species in the South for over 200 years and is commercially important for lumber and pulpwood; in restoration contexts it is a key species for re-establishing mixed pine-hardwood forests on former agricultural and cutover lands in the Ozarks and upper Gulf Coastal Plain. Grow in full sun in any well-drained, acidic soil; it is more drought-tolerant than most other southern pines and one of the hardiest (to zone 5) of the typically warm-climate southern pines.
